title: PerfectLoop: Looping Worlds and the Ghosts of Old Interfaces
summary: Anonymous artist PerfectLoop builds looping animations from the ghosts of old computer interfaces — Windows 98 dialogs, green-screen terminals, MIDI panels — using Blender and TouchDesigner. A former sound designer exiled from indie games for his crypto enthusiasm, he stumbled into visual art making an album cover; the visuals “ate the whole practice.” His rule: “If it’s not a fuck yes, it’s a no.” Old interfaces are “pinned to a map of time” — fixed, weighty — while newer ones haven’t settled yet.

title: Marisol Vengas: Art as Social Engagement
summary: Pseudonym of Chico-based artist Max Infeld, whose Curio Cards 27, 28, and 29 are among the first known generative artworks on Ethereum. Vengas hosted ad-hoc art-making sessions at a local café where passing strangers would contribute to the composition — each finished piece touched by many unrelated hands, a story written by the crowd. A computer-science student turned Fine Arts graduate, Infeld created community currencies predating Bitcoin and brought participatory art into the blockchain era.

title: Adam Berninger: The Truth of Generative Art
summary: Curator Adam Berninger’s Feral File exhibition “Truth” brought together seven generative artists — Tyler Boswell, Presstube x bzor, Ella Hoeppner, Charlotte Dann, David Seven, HAL09999, and Lisa Orth — to explore scale, abstraction, and inner truth. Inspired by Charles and Ray Eames’s 1977 film Powers of Ten, Berninger asked each artist to “obscure a specific perception of size,” revealing a common fabric across cosmic and microscopic scales. “There’s no wrong way to interpret an abstract work,” he says, “and so they’re all right, and therefore true.”

title: Kjetil Golid: Algorithms as Creative Expression
summary: Norwegian generative artist and former system developer whose Velocity Series collaboration with Oracle Red Bull Racing and Bybit yielded two NFT collections: Decision Matrix — 377 algorithmically generated pieces encoding each Red Bull race as interlocking cogwheels and chip stacks — and Risk/Reward, a generative exploration of Formula 1's high-stakes dynamics where calculated risk and potential reward define the thin line between victory and defeat.

title: Spells of Genesis: The First Blockchain Trading Card Game
summary: Beginning in 2015 on Bitcoin's Counterparty protocol, Spells of Genesis was the first on-chain economy game ever made — a trading card game that predated the NFT boom by years and pioneered the concept of blockchain-based digital collectibles.

title: Harvey Rayner: Algorithmic Synesthesia at Art Basel
summary: Art Blocks talks with generative artist Harvey Rayner about his project Algorithmic Synesthesia, debuting at Art Basel's Zero 10. A Web3-based artist working at the intersection of algorithmic artistry, community building, and blockchain innovation, Rayner explores how code can translate sensory experience into visual form.

title: PridePunks 2018: A Political Art Statement for Inclusion
summary: Created by Dennison Bertram during Pride Month 2018, PridePunks is the first-ever CryptoPunks derivative — a political art statement that wrapped the iconic pixel PFP inside a rainbow banner. “The Rainbow felt like the most unambiguous statement that all would be welcome,” Bertram said. After an initial buggy launch that integrated only two CryptoPunks, the contract was revived in 2022 alongside a DAO dedicated to funding LGBTQ+ inclusion and underrepresented voices in Web3.

title: CryptoPunks: The V1 Bug, the Heist, and the Birth of the PFP NFT
summary: Launched in June 2017 by Larva Labs, CryptoPunks were among the first NFTs on Ethereum — 10,000 free-to-claim 24×24 pixel characters. The original V1 contract carried a critical bug: buyers could withdraw the ETH they paid, effectively stealing the Punk. User “hemba” exploited the flaw, heisting dozens before Larva Labs patched the contract on 23 June 2017, re-minting all 10,000 Punks and airdropping them back. The V1 Punks survive as wrapped historical artifacts — the flawed but authentic birthplace of the PFP NFT.

title: Aleksandra Jovanić: Computer Scientist Turned Generative Artist
summary: Aleksandra Jovanić is an artist and programmer from Belgrade, Serbia, who holds a Doctorate in Digital Arts and a BSc in Computer Science. Influenced by Sol LeWitt's conceptualism, her work — including The Anatomy of Fracture series on fxhash — draws on minimalist principles to explore communication, miscommunication, and the unpredictable beauty of algorithmic systems.

title: Kim Asendorf: From Pixel to Paradigm
summary: German visual artist Kim Asendorf is renowned for creating the open-source pixel-sorting algorithm that thousands of artists have used since 2012. A pioneer of generative and glitch art, he bridges conceptual strategies with algorithmic systems to create abstract animations and images. Featured in eight group exhibitions in 2023 and the youngest artist to produce work for the Venet Foundation's annual exhibition.

title: Melissa Wiederrecht: Generative Pattern and Color Mastery
summary: Based in Saudi Arabia, Melissa Wiederrecht is universally recognized as one of the pre-eminent generative artists working today. With two Art Blocks releases and multiple collections on fxhash (Zbageti, Solitude, Orbs), her work is characterized by masterful use of line, color, and algorithmic precision.

title: Per Kristian Stoveland: Pursuit and the Velocity Series
summary: Norwegian generative artist Per Kristian Stoveland’s Velocity Series collections — Pursuit and Echo of Intensity — were commissioned by Oracle Red Bull Racing and Bybit. Pursuit channels the spectral beauty of open-shutter night photography: streaks of light carving through dark Norwegian-inspired landscapes, with F1 track geometries (curves, chicanes, parabolicas) embedded as algorithmic trait systems. A self-taught coder who transitioned from conventional art in 2021, Stoveland lets coding anomalies guide his process — relinquishing exact expectations to let the algorithm surprise him.

title: 10 Digital Artists: The Reign of Generative Art
artist: Ivona Tau, Sarah Ridgley, Kaoru Tanaka, Marcelo Soria Rodríguez, Emily Xie, Artemis, Anna Lucia, Studio Moodsoup, Melissa Wiederrecht, Shunsuke Takawo
summary: A survey of 10 generative artists shaping the field: Ivona Tau (AI-generated universal memories), Sarah Ridgley (algorithmic botanicals), Kaoru Tanaka (code-driven calligraphy), Marcelo Soria Rodríguez (data sonification), Emily Xie (textile-inspired algorithms), Artemis (glitch aesthetics), Anna Lucia (generative identity), Studio Moodsoup / Stefan Reyniers (child-like wonder through minimalist geometry), Melissa Wiederrecht (pattern and color mastery), and Shunsuke Takawo (daily code sketches). Together they map the expanding terrain where code, chance, and human intent converge.

title: The Memes by 6529: Spreading Decentralization Through Art
artist: Killian Moore, Michael Hirshon, Camibus, threepanelcrimes
summary: A multi-season NFT project by the pseudonymous crypto philosopher punk6529, who aims to onboard 100 million people to NFTs. The Memes collection leverages visual art to promote principles of decentralization, self-sovereignty, and an open metaverse — featuring collaborations with artists like XCOPY alongside community creators.

title: Alex Grasser: Architectures of Code and Elasticity
summary: Alexander Grasser-Parger is an architect, lecturer, researcher, and generative artist whose work explores real-time participatory digital design. His fxhash collections — Redundancy, Polyvalence, and Elasticity — investigate the ambiguity of discrete continuity in architecture, treating generative systems as resilient, self-assembling structures.

title: Cryptograffiti: Bitcoin Activism Through Art
summary: A Bitcoin activist artist who repurposes banking materials in his work, Cryptograffiti contributed three iconic cards to the historic Curio Cards project including BTC Keys, Mine Bitcoin, and BTC. He was the first artist Curio Cards founder Travis Uhrig met who made art about Bitcoin — helping others understand what the technology meant.

title: Art Blocks: Building the Home for Generative Art on Chain
summary: Founded in 2020 by Erick Calderon (Snowfro) with the release of Chromie Squiggle, Art Blocks became the premier platform for on-chain generative art. The platform lets artists deploy algorithms that generate unique artworks at the moment of minting — a model that revolutionized how digital art is created, collected, and valued.

title: Curio Cards: The First Art NFTs on Ethereum
summary: Launched on May 9, 2017 — six weeks before CryptoPunks — Curio Cards is recognized as the first art NFT project on Ethereum. A complete set sold for $1.27 million at Christie's in October 2021. Features 30 cards by seven artists including Cryptograffiti's iconic BTC Keys.

title: Harm van den Dorpel: The First Artist to Sell Blockchain Art to a Museum
summary: In 2015, Dutch artist Harm van den Dorpel became the first artist to sell artwork authenticated via blockchain to a museum — the MAK in Vienna acquired 'Event Listeners' using Bitcoin. A co-founder of left.gallery, he treats generative systems as a medium for thinking about mutation, decay, and distributed authorship.

title: Rare Pepe: Bitcoin's Original Crypto Art Movement
summary: Before the term 'NFT' existed, Rare Pepe launched in 2016 on Bitcoin's Counterparty protocol. Created by an open-source collective of 'Pepe Scientists,' the 36-series collection demonstrated the potential of blockchain-verified digital art years before Ethereum NFTs took off.

title: MoonCats: The Rediscovered 2017 NFT Treasure
summary: Deployed in August 2017, MoonCats was one of the first generative art projects on Ethereum. After years of dormancy, NFT archaeologists rediscovered the project in 2021, triggering a rescue frenzy for all 25,600 pixel cats — including ultra-rare Genesis MoonCats.
